result:
TCP connection-oriented, UDP fire-and-forget.

TCP does handshake (SYN/SYN-ACK/ACK), guarantees delivery, orders packets, retransmits losses, flow control, congestion control. Stream abstraction.

UDP just sends datagrams. No handshake, no ack, no ordering, no retransmit. Packet may arrive, may not, may arrive twice, may arrive out of order. You handle it.

TCP for correctness: HTTP, SSH, file transfer, DB protocols.
UDP for speed/latency tolerance of loss: DNS, video/voice, games, QUIC base, mDNS.

TCP header ~20 bytes, UDP header 8 bytes. TCP has more state per connection on both ends; UDP stateless at transport layer.

QUIC worth noting — runs over UDP but rebuilds TCP-like reliability in userspace with better multiplexing and 0-RTT.

UDP just sends datagrams. No handshake, no ack, no ordering, no retransmit. Packet may arrive, may not, may arrive twice, may arrive out of order. You handle it.

TCP for correctness: HTTP, SSH, file transfer, DB protocols.
UDP for speed/latency tolerance of loss: DNS, video/voice, games, QUIC base, mDNS.

TCP header ~20 bytes, UDP header 8 bytes. TCP has more state per connection on both ends; UDP stateless at transport layer.
